content.problemStatement:
Chef is standing in the centre of a circle. The perimeter of the circle is divided into hhh units (numbered 111 through hhh) and some of those units contain buildings. There are NNN buildings in total (numbered 111 through NNN); let's denote the position of building iii, i.e. the number of the unit containing this building, by pip_ipi‚Äč. Note that a unit may contain more than one building.

Chef has a vision range equal to xxx. This means he can look at an arbitrary contiguous range of xxx units at the same time.

Chef likes to play with his girlfriend Cheftanya. Since he belongs to Wakanda, he decided to play a shooting game. In this game, he is going to look in some direction and shoot all the buildings he sees (without looking in any other direction). Cheftanya chooses the direction in which he looks; since she loves mathematics, she is going to choose this direction randomly using a special probability density function defined below. Before playing the game, though, she is interested in the expected number of buildings Chef will shoot. Chef is going to be busy shooting, so she asks for your help. Can you impress Cheftanya with your skills?

This expected value can be written as a fraction P/QP/QP/Q, where PPP and QQQ are coprime. Since we are in the 21st century, nobody is interested in fractions. Therefore, you should compute P‚čÖQ‚ąí1P\cdot Q^{-1}P‚čÖQ‚ąí1 modulo 163577857163577857163577857, where Q‚ąí1Q^{-1}Q‚ąí1 denotes the modular inverse of QQQ modulo 163577857163577857163577857. (It is guaranteed that this inverse exists and is unique.)

Consider a sequence of integers a1,a2,‚Ä¶,aha_1, a_2, \dots, a_ha1‚Äč,a2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,ah‚Äč; let's denote s=‚ąĎi=1hais=\sum_{i=1}^h a_is=‚ąĎi=1h‚Äčai‚Äč. We guarantee that sss is not divisible by respective modulo (163577857163577857163577857).
For each iii (1‚Č§i‚Č§h1 \le i \le h1‚Č§i‚Č§h), the probability that Cheftanya directs Chef to look at units i,i+1,‚Ä¶,i+x‚ąí1i, i+1, \dots, i+x-1i,i+1,‚Ä¶,i+x‚ąí1 is ai/sa_i / sai‚Äč/s; the units are cyclic, so we can consider "unit iii" to be equivalent to "unit i‚ąíhi-hi‚ąíh". Since the sequence aaa can be very large, you are only given its first KKK elements. You are also given a sequence c1,c2,‚Ä¶,cKc_1, c_2, \dots, c_Kc1‚Äč,c2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,cK‚Äč; the remainder of sequence aaa can be generated using the formula
ai=‚ąĎj=1Kcj‚čÖai‚ąíj‚ąÄ‚ÄČi:‚ÄÖ‚ÄäK<i‚Č§h‚ÄČ.a_i = \sum_{j=1}^K c_j\cdot a_{i-j} \quad \forall\,i:\;K \lt i \le h\,.ai‚Äč=‚ąĎj=1K‚Äčcj‚Äč‚čÖai‚ąíj‚Äč‚ąÄi:K<i‚Č§h.
constraints: 1‚Č§pi‚Č§h1 \le p_i \le h1‚Č§pi‚Äč‚Č§h for each valid iii
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer NNN denoting the number of buildings.
The second line contains a single integer hhh denoting the total number of units the circle is divided into.
The third line contains a single integer xxx denoting Chef's vision range.
The fourth line contains a single integer KKK.
The fifth line contains NNN space-separated integers p1,p2,‚Ä¶,pNp_1, p_2, \dots, p_Np1‚Äč,p2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,pN‚Äč denoting the positions of the buildings.
The sixth line contains KKK space-separated integers a1,a2,‚Ä¶,aKa_1, a_2, \dots, a_Ka1‚Äč,a2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,aK‚Äč.
The seventh line contains KKK space-separated integers c1,c2,‚Ä¶,cKc_1, c_2, \dots, c_Kc1‚Äč,c2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,cK‚Äč denoting the coefficients for generating the remaining probabilities.
content.outputFormat: Print a single line containing one integer ‚ÄĒ the number equivalent to the expected number of buildings modulo 163577857163577857163577857.
